query: is gamma-H2AX a reliable marker for detecting double strand breaks in human cells?

retrieved_docs.title: Fanconi Anemia -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Studies
retrieved_docs.content: The chromosomal breakage/stress cytogenetics test is a diagnostic test indicated in those with severe pancytopenia defined as an absolute neutrophil count of <100 mL, hemoglobin <10 g/dL, reticulocyte <40,000 mL, bone marrow cellularity <25%, and a platelet count of <50,000 mL. Testing with DNA cross-linkers such as diepoxybutane or mitomycin C stimulates the breakage of DNA in the absence of a DNA repair system. These agents increase the chromatids' breaks, gaps, reduplications, or rearrangements. Cultured fibroblasts also show chromosome fragility and are preferred in patients with negative T-lymphocyte chromosome fragility testing or those who have already undergone hematopoietic cell transplantation. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Chromosome Instability Syndromes -- Evaluation -- Fanconi Anemia
retrieved_docs.content: Investigation for Fanconi anemia includes chromosomal stress testing and next-generation sequencing panels. Chromosomal stress testing involves the assessment of chromosomal breakage in T-lymphocytes from peripheral blood upon exposure of cells to diepoxybutane or mitomycin C. The test is sensitive but not specific because other rare genetic diseases can also show breakage. Flow cytometry, which assesses cell cycle analysis upon exposure to DNA cross-linking agents, is another helpful test in evaluating this condition. In Franconi anemia, cells cannot repair DNA damage and undergo cell cycle arrest in G2, leading to a higher percentage of cells in G2. Gene sequencing for this condition is generally used as a confirmatory tool for patients with positive breakage studies. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, DNA Repair -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: DNA repair is crucial to correcting the mistakes our replicative machinery makes when forming new materials within our bodies. Damage to DNA molecules, if left unrepaired, can often lead to carcinogenesis, which is the development of cancer cells in the affected area that can easily spread and replicate through mitosis. Conversely, cancer developments can be understood and analyzed through the mechanisms in which DNA damage occurs, and repair processes are affected as a result of the carcinogens in the body. This process has received extensive study relative to exogenous damage such as ionizing radiation that can lead to leukemia and melanoma due to various exposures, ranging from simple ultraviolet to X-ray.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, DNA Repair -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Non-homologous end-joining is essential before the broken DNA replicates, as a double-strand break usually leads to cell death. It uses micro homologies, which are short homologous sequences on the end of individual strands of DNA, to join the two fractured ends back together by translocation. However, if there are base deletions at the break site, the rejoining of the strands can lead to mutations in the genome. Harmful X-rays or carcinogens can cause such breaks, resulting in the gross rearrangement of chromosomes. Burkitt’s lymphoma is an example of cancer arising due to translocation. BRCA1 and BRCA2 proteins are involved in the homologous repair, and mutations in these genes are known to cause breast cancer. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Fanconi Anemia -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Cell cycle analysis/flow cytometry is an alternative to the chromosomal breakage test. In this test, those cells with impaired DNA repair undergo G2 arrest following DNA cross-linking agent exposure. Immunoblotting of GANCD2 mutation is less commonly used. Fanconi anemia gene sequencing is recommended in patients with positive chromosomal breakage testing. The identification of genetic defects is confirmatory and excludes other chromosome breakage syndromes. Prenatally, abnormal chromosome breakage can be analyzed with amniotic fluid cells or chorionic villous biopsy. An elevated level of serum alpha-protein is a rapid screening diagnostic test, but it may not be identified in many cases. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Xeroderma Pigmentosum -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Unscheduled DNA synthesis involves exposing the patient’s cultured fibroblasts to UV radiation and then assessing their ability to repair DNA. DNA repair after UV exposure is different than DNA synthesis during normal cell replication and is therefore called unscheduled DNA synthesis. The amount of UDS after irradiation is quantifiable by determining the number of nucleotides incorporated into DNA. Nucleotide incorporation into DNA is determined using one of the following methods: fluorescence assay, autoradiography, or liquid scintillation counting. If the patient’s level of UDS is low following UV exposure, the diagnosis of xeroderma pigmentosum is confirmed. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, DNA Repair -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: Damage inflicted on the DNA during or after replication and transcription are not to be confused with mutations. DNA damage results in single and double-strand breakages and is repairable by the different actions of enzymes in the cell. This is because the enzymes have a template for repair present in either the complementary strand of a damaged DNA molecule or a homologous chromosome that can be copied and used to implement the correct base pairs and base orders in the double helix.

retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Apoptosis -- Clinical Pathology -- DNA Fragmentation
retrieved_docs.content: A hallmark of late-stage apoptosis is the breakdown of DNA into 180 to 200 bp fragments. The TUNEL (terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase dUTP nick end labeling) assay commonly labels 3'-OH DNA ends with fluorescent markers, allowing detection through microscopy or flow cytometry. Alternatively, DNA laddering through agarose gel electrophoresis offers a more affordable but less sensitive option. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Cell -- Clinical Significance -- DNA Mutation
retrieved_docs.content: Mitotic errors can result from prolonged exposure to radiation, particularly nuclear radiation, as well as certain chemicals and drugs. However, some cells, such as neural and cardiac cells, do not undergo mitosis and instead remain in the Go phase . [51]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, DNA Repair -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Novel methods are under development to target DNA repair pathways that have suffered mutagenetic effects and use them to fight the tumor to induce malignant cell death. DNA repair can be recruited to the carcinogens benefit, so the goal of this area of research is to turn the repair pathways back on the tumor and induce its degradation. [11] [12] [13] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Therapy -- Definition/Introduction -- Physical and Biological Principles of Radiation
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation takes advantage of cancer cells because these cells usually have impaired DNA repair mechanisms, in contrast to normal cells, which can rapidly repair double-stranded breaks. Therefore, a fractionated approach, i.e., splitting the total radiation dose over multiple daily treatments, is typically used, such that DNA damage in normal cells is repaired between treatments, while damage to cancer cells accumulates over time, causing preferential cancer cell death.  Both the dose per fraction and the total dose affect tumor and normal tissue response. In general, the lower the daily dose of radiation, the less likely it is to cause toxicity, but only specific cell lines (such as myeloma or lymphoma) are susceptible to these relatively low daily doses. Therefore, a balance needs to be achieved between daily doses low enough to spare normal tissue but high enough to cause cancer cell death. For many cancers, a dose of 180 to 200 cGy per day is used. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Wound Assessment -- Clinical Significance -- Chronic
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation Therapy Causes stasis/occlusion of small vessels and damages fibroblasts and nuclei

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, DNA Repair -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: Ionizing radiations such as X-rays can dissociate water molecules in the cell, producing hydroxyl radicals that can react with DNA, altering its structure and cause DNA strands to break. Certain toxins and drugs cause chemical associations resulting in single or double-strand breakage in the DNA helix. Thermal disruption via DNA being exposed to high temperatures causes higher rates of depurination to occur, as well as single-strand breaks. Radiation exposure also causes strand breaks. These types of induced damage are very severe and can cause irreparable damage and harm to the cell. Similarly, environmental toxins such as cigarette smoke contain carcinogens like benzo-pyrene. These chemicals can be oxidized by cellular enzymes and cause DNA damage by forming DNA adducts.

retrieved_docs.title: Chromosome Instability Syndromes -- Evaluation -- Nijmegen Breakage Syndrome
retrieved_docs.content: Investigation for Nijmegen breakage syndrome focuses on the assessment of immunodeficiency. Tests include assessment of immunoglobulin levels, cluster of differentiation (CD) 4, CD8, CD19, CD57, and class switching of memory B cells. Karyotyping sometimes shows structural chromosomal aberrations in T= lymphocytes at chromosomes 7 and 14. There is also sensitivity to ionizing radiation. There are also mutations in the NBN gene and the absence of fibrin protein. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Carcinogenesis -- Biochemical and Genetic Pathology -- DNA Damage
retrieved_docs.content: DNA damage can result from endogenous factors, extrinsic factors, or a combination of both. Endogenous factors include normal metabolic processes, such as oxidative stress, while extrinsic factors encompass environmental exposures, such as radiation and toxins.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1437
retrieved_docs.content: In radiation biology, each qualifier can modulate, enhance, mitigate, or otherwise influence physiological responses to ionizing and non‑ionizing radiation. Hypoxanthine’s PARP‑inhibitory activity specifically suppresses DNA‑repair mechanisms, affecting cellular recovery after radiation‑induced damage. Recognizing these agents as radiation modifiers informs the design of protective strategies, therapeutic regimens, and risk assessments for both therapeutic radiation exposure and accidental radiation incidents. Collectively, the inclusion of these permissible qualifiers facilitates standardized manufacturing, regulatory compliance, targeted therapeutic development, and a comprehensive understanding of immunological, metabolic, and radiobiological processes, thereby improving patient care and advancing biomedical research.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1803
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Other and nonspecific abnormal cytological, histological, immunological and DNA test findings” includes specific abnormal results such as a positive culture (المزرعة إيجابية) and an abnormal chromosome analysis (تحليل الكروموسوم غير طبيعي). These particular findings are considered sub‑categories of the broader group of nonspecific abnormal laboratory results, indicating that when such tests return these outcomes, they represent distinct types of abnormal cytological, histological, immunological, or DNA evidence that require further clinical interpretation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1886
retrieved_docs.content: these compounds function as “allowed qualifiers” in the context of ionizing radiation because they can modify biological responses to radiation—either enhancing radiation‑induced damage, altering tissue radiosensitivity, or providing radioprotective or radiosensitizing effects. Awareness of these dual roles informs the integration of the agents into diagnostic imaging, radiotherapy planning, occupational radiation safety and broader radioprotection strategies, thereby optimizing therapeutic outcomes and patient safety.

retrieved_docs.content: employed in multiple chemotherapy regimens and targeted‑therapy protocols for the treatment of Hodgkin lymphoma, providing disease control, remission induction and survival benefit through mechanisms that include alkylation, DNA intercalation, microtubule inhibition, antibody‑mediated cytotoxicity, immune checkpoint blockade and cytokine support. The systematic categorization of these agents under the GDC property of Pharmacologic Substance underscores their clinical relevance for decision‑making and patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: Other and nonspecific abnormal cytological, histological, immunological and DNA test findings encompass a broad spectrum of atypical laboratory results, and within this category a positive culture (المزرعة إيجابية) is identified as a specific child finding.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2881
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells that cannot be definitively classified as benign or malignant are considered abnormal cells in the context of ovarian cancer, indicating that such indeterminate tumor cells represent the pathological cellular component of this disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2803
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells are the abnormal cellular component that defines ovarian cancer, indicating that ovarian cancer is characterized by the presence of these malignant tumor cells.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2740
retrieved_docs.content: PARP inhibitory agents are classified as hypoxanthine derivatives, indicating that they belong to the chemical class of hypoxanthine and function as inhibitors of poly(ADP‑ribose) polymerase. This classification highlights the structural relationship between PARP inhibitory compounds and the hypoxanthine scaffold, which is relevant for understanding their mechanism of action and potential therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2813
retrieved_docs.content: Other and nonspecific abnormal cytological, histological, immunological and DNA test findings represent a broad category of atypical laboratory results, and within this category the specific finding تحليل الكروموسوم غير طبيعي (abnormal chromosome analysis) is included as a child or sub‑type, indicating that abnormal chromosome analysis is one of the nonspecific abnormal test results encompassed by this broader classification.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2867
retrieved_docs.content: Other and nonspecific abnormal cytological, histological, immunological and DNA test findings are classified within the broader domain of immunizations and screening for infectious disease, indicating that these atypical laboratory results are considered part of preventive health assessments. Within this category, the specific test known as تحليل الكروموسوم غير طبيعي (abnormal chromosome analysis) is identified as a child concept, representing a particular type of cytogenetic evaluation that falls under the umbrella of other and nonspecific abnormal test findings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 273
retrieved_docs.content: Anthracyclines and related substances constitute a class of antineoplastic agents that includes the individual drugs aklarubicin, epirubicin, idarubicin, and mitoxantron. These compounds share structural and pharmacologic characteristics typical of anthracycline chemotherapy, such as intercalation into DNA and inhibition of topoisomerase II, which contribute to their cytotoxic effects against rapidly dividing cancer cells. The ET protocol, a therapeutic regimen used in oncology, is derived from or mapped to epirubicin, indicating that epirubicin serves as the foundational agent for this specific treatment protocol. Understanding that aklarubicin, epirubicin, idarubicin, and mitoxantron belong to the anthracycline family helps clinicians select appropriate regimens and anticipate class‑related toxicities, while the linkage of the ET protocol to epirubicin guides its application in patient care.
